objective: Describe the general steps involved in synthesizing aspirin from salicylic acid in a university chemistry lab.
final_response:
The conversion of **salicylic acid** (2-hydroxybenzoic acid) into **aspirin** (acetylsalicylic acid) is a classic **nucleophilic acyl substitution** reaction. The phenolic hydroxyl group (–OH) of salicylic acid acts as the nucleophile, attacking acetic anhydride (the acylating agent).

### Overall Reaction
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{OH})\text{COOH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{OCOCH}_3)\text{COOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$

**Reagents:** Acetic anhydride  
**Catalyst:** Concentrated sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) or phosphoric acid ($\text{H}_3\text{PO}_4$) – optional but accelerates the reaction  
**Byproduct:** Acetic acid

---

### Detailed Mechanism

The mechanism proceeds via a **tetrahedral intermediate**. An acid catalyst (if used) increases the electrophilicity of the acetic anhydride carbonyl carbon by protonation.

#### **Step 1: Activation (Acid Catalysis)**
The acid catalyst protonates one of the carbonyl oxygens of acetic anhydride. This increases the partial positive charge on the carbonyl carbon, making it more susceptible to nucleophilic attack.

$$(\text{CH}_3\text{CO
